sections.title: Inversions with negative adverbial words and phrases

content.subtitle: Form
items: **No sooner had I booked** the flight than they told me it had been cancelled.

rows.label: Never
items: **Never have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: Rarely
items: **Rarely have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: Seldom
items: **Seldom have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: No sooner (… than)
items: **No sooner had Matt arrived** at the airport **than** he missed his flight.

rows.label: Hardly (… when)
items: **Hardly had Matt arrived** at the airport **when** he missed his flight.

rows.label: Not only (… but also/too)
items: **Not only were you** late for the departure, **but** you had **also** forgotten your passport.

rows.label: Under no circumstances
items: **Under no circumstances are passengers allowed** to leave the gate area without permission.

rows.label: At no time/point
items: **At no time/point was I told** what the tour would involve.

rows.label: Little
items: **Little did I realise** that I would end up visiting fifteen countries just two years later.

rows.label: Not until
items: **Not until the next morning did I hear** that my lost luggage had been found.

rows.label: Only
items: **Only at the end of the trek did I think** I had a chance of reaching the summit.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: *Little* is used with verbs of thought (*realise*, *know*, *suspect*, etc) and means 'I did not realise/etc at all'.
✓ **Little did I know** how challenging the journey was going to be.
(= I did not know at all how challenging the journey was going to be.)
With *not until* and *only*, you have to be careful about which verb to invert.
✓ **Not until I had packed** my bags **was I allowed** to leave the hostel.
✗ Not until had I packed my bags was I allowed to leave the hostel.
✓ **Only when I had packed** my bags **was I allowed** to leave the hostel.
✗ Only when had I packed my bags I was allowed to leave the hostel.

rows.label: In short answers and other similar structures using *so*, *neither* and *nor*
items: 'I'm a tour guide.' 'Really? **So am I**!'
My sister doesn't enjoy long-haul flights, and **neither do I / nor do I**.

rows.label: After *as*, *so* and *such*
items: The guide was nervous about the group's arrival, **as were the rest of the porters**.
**So late was it** that there was no one left at the campsite.
**Such a hot day was it** that no one wanted to continue hiking.

rows.label: In conditional sentences
items: **Were our equipment** better maintained, we might have a safer expedition. (= If our equipment were better maintained ...)
**Had I known** about the detour, I would have taken a different route. (= If I had known ...)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Remember that '*So am/do/have I*' is used to agree with a positive statement and '*Neither/Nor am/do/have I*' is used to agree with a negative statement.
examples: ✓ 'I really don't enjoy long layovers.' '**Neither do I / Nor do I**.'

rows.label: We use 's with singular nouns, including names, and with irregular plurals which do not end in -s.
items: That's the tour guide**'s** map, over there.
We are still waiting for Sarah**'s** decision about which route to take.
I think that women**'s** rights should be respected everywhere.

rows.label: We just add an apostrophe to regular plural nouns ending in -s.
items: The travellers**'** bags were checked at customs.

rows.label: We use 's or s' in some time expressions.
items: After an hour**'s** walk, I finally reached the scenic viewpoint.
I'll be at the hostel in about ten minutes**'** time.

rows.label: We usually use 's or s' with people (or groups composed of people) and animals. For other things, we normally use *of the/my/etc*.
items: Is this John**'s** backpack?
We held a meeting to discuss the agency**'s** itinerary.
The vet had a close look at the cat**'s** paw.
The mechanic had a close look at the frame **of my** bicycle. (not ...at my bicycle's frame)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: With singular names ending in -s, some people use 's and some people just add an apostrophe. Both are correct, although using 's is more common.
✓ The itinerary is on Charles**'s** tablet in the tent.
✓ The itinerary is on Charles**'** tablet in the tent.
With plural names ending in -s, we just add an apostrophe, as with other plurals.
✓ The Smiths**'** travel agency eventually closed down.
With phrases, the possessive 's and s' must go at the end of the whole phrase.
✓ Tom, Dick and Harry**'s** backpack is around here somewhere.
✗ Tom's, Dick's and Harry's backpack is around here somewhere.
✓ The President of France**'s** visit to England will be good for tourism.
✗ The President's of France visit to England will be good for tourism.

rows.label: Possessive determiners (*my*, *your*, *his*, *her*, *its*, *our*, *their*) come before a noun and show possession
items: I'm really excited about **my** upcoming adventure.
Are you looking forward to meeting **your** new travel companions?

rows.label: Possessive pronouns (*mine*, *yours*, *his*, *hers*, *ours*, *yours*, *theirs*) are used instead of a noun
items: My trip was amazing. How about **yours**? (= ...your trip)
Our journey is going well, but I hear Simon and Julie are having problems with **theirs**. (= ...their journey)

rows.label: Possessive pronouns can also be used after *of* to show possession
items: She's a fellow traveller of **mine**. (= ...one of my fellow travellers)
Are they tour guests of **yours**?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: A common mistake is using a possessive pronoun instead of a possessive determiner or using a determiner instead of a pronoun.
✓ I don't really get on so well with **my** travel partner.
✗ I don't really get on so well with **mine** travel partner.
✓ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **theirs**?
✗ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **their**?
Remember that there are no apostrophes in possessive pronouns.
✗ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **their's**?
Don't get confused between *its* (possessive determiner, without an apostrophe) and *it's* (contraction for *it is* or *it has*, with an apostrophe).
✓ Our travel agency is hoping to increase **its** share of the market.
✗ Our travel agency is hoping to increase **it's** share of the market.

items.sentence: Hardly had we started our hike **when/than** it began to rain heavily.
No sooner had we set off from the camp **when/than** my backpack strap broke.
No sooner had the tourists reached the viewpoint **when/than** clouds covered the mountains.
Hardly had I closed my eyes in the tent **when/than** I heard strange noises outside.
No sooner do I unpack my bags **when/than** it is time to move to the next destination.
Hardly were we at the summit **when/than** a fierce storm began to approach.
No sooner ...... in my seat on the plane than the pilot announced a long delay.
Never ...... a more breathtaking view in all my travels.
At no time ...... that the mountain pass would be so dangerous.
Little ...... that I had left my passport back at the hotel.
Not until ...... the Northern Lights did I realise how stunning they truly were.
Not only ...... my passport, I've lost my boarding pass, too.
Rarely ...... about local customs enough to learn even a few words of the language.
Only when ...... may passengers cross the border checkpoint.
options: A is the green light on
B the green light is on
C did the green light be on
D on is the green light

items.sentence: Seldom ...... such a welcoming host at a travel guesthouse!
Only after completing the safety briefing ...... to begin the guided dive.

sections.title: C: Error correction (Inversions)
sections.instructions: Write **OK** if the bold word or phrase is correct. If it is incorrect, write the correct version.

items.sentence: Hardly **Tom had** opened his tent when he heard a strange noise outside.
Only when I reached the summit did I realise why the hike was so special.
Not only **you passed** the border checkpoint, but you also got your travel permit approved!
Not until **do I see** the weather forecast tomorrow will I know whether the trek is safe.
No sooner had we found our campsite than the rain began.
At no point while the climb is in progress **members of the public are not allowed** onto the restricted path.
items.answer: are members of the public allowed

items.sentence: Under no circumstances **do the equipment** to be used on the glacier without a qualified guide.
items.answer: is the equipment to be used

items.sentence: Little **I thought** that one day this forgotten trail would become the most popular hiking route in the region.
Never **did I be** so grateful to find a warm bed after such a long and exhausting journey.
Hardly **I had put** up the tent when the storm rolled in.

sections.title: D: Complete using inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in brackets.

items.sentence: No sooner (1) (I / start) ...... working at Trailblazers Tours, the adventure travel company, than I realised it was the perfect base for my hobby: exploring off-limits destinations!
Don't get me wrong. At no point (2) (I / have) ...... ever put my fellow travellers in danger.
Not only (3) (I / like) ...... pushing boundaries, but sometimes (4) (it / help) ...... the company to develop new and exciting routes.
Rarely (5) (they / see) ...... it like that, of course.
I decided I would try to access the restricted wilderness reserve. Little (6) (I / realise) ...... what was waiting for me.
On the second day of a group hike, hardly (7) (everyone else / leave) ...... when I started looking for unmarked trails through the jungle.
Seldom (8) (this / is) ...... enough, though. You usually have to venture much further than that.
Not until midnight (9) (I / get) ...... past the entrance to the restricted zone.
Hardly (10) (I / have) ...... time to look around the area when the door to my cabin flew open and three rangers rushed in.
Only when (11) (they / show) ...... me their badges (12) (I / realise) ...... that they were park officials.
They had been tracking my GPS signal for days! Not only (13) (I / be fired) ......, but (14) (they / keep) ...... all my expedition records as evidence, too.

sections.title: E: Inversion transformations
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence: Photographs are not permitted at the sacred site under any circumstances.
items.answer: Under no circumstances are photographs permitted.

items.sentence: Travelling together, we have never faced such a serious problem as this.
items.answer: Never before have we faced such a serious problem as this.

items.sentence: At the crowded station, I only realised it was Vanessa when she removed her hat.
items.answer: Only when Vanessa removed her hat did I realise it was her.

items.sentence: The audience didn't laugh at any point during the cultural show.
items.answer: At no point during the show did the audience laugh.

items.sentence: As soon as Patrick had finished the booking e-mail, he sent it.
items.answer: No sooner had Patrick finished the e-mail than he sent it.

items.sentence: Watching the match at the hostel bar, it wasn't clear who was going to win until the last few minutes.
items.answer: Not until the last few minutes was it clear who was going to win the match.

items.sentence: Dennis had just passed his driving test when he had an accident on the way to the airport.
items.answer: Hardly had Dennis passed his driving test when he had an accident.

items.sentence: While staying in a remote mountain village, I didn't learn the result of the match until I read the evening paper.
items.answer: Only when I read the evening paper did I learn the result of the match.

items.sentence: Despite public pressure, the president will not resign under any circumstances.
items.answer: Under no circumstances will the president resign.

items.sentence: The backpacker, who was the accused, showed no emotion at any time during the trial.
items.answer: At no time during the trial did the accused show any emotion.

sections.title: F: Short answers (So/Neither/Nor)
sections.instructions: Complete the responses to these statements.

items.sentence: 'I'd love to travel around the world one day.' 'So .......'
'We never seem to stay long enough in one place.' 'Neither .......'
'I'm not joining the guided tour tomorrow.' 'Nor .......'
'I didn't book my accommodation in advance.' 'Nor .......'
'I'll be trekking through the mountains this time next week.' 'So .......'
'I had no idea the waterfall was so stunning.' 'Neither .......'
'I should really invest in better hiking boots.' 'So .......'

sections.title: G: Inversions with as/so/such
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Such a long way ...... it that we decided to take the train rather than walk.
So poorly ...... I read maps that I almost never agree to be the group navigator.
Hikers are expected to stick to the marked paths, as ...... cyclists.
So exhausted ...... I after the long trek that I decided to rest for an entire day.
Such a danger to fellow trekkers ...... you that the guide has no choice but to send you back.
So cold ...... it at the mountain pass that all our water supplies froze overnight.
Photography is banned inside the sacred cave, as ...... loud music and open fires.
I have been having extraordinary travel experiences lately, as ...... my travelling companion.

sections.title: H: Form possessives
sections.instructions: Form possessives from the nouns given.

items.sentence: (Billy): ...... backpack
(my mum): ...... travel stories
(our next door neighbours): ...... holiday cottage
(George the Fifth): ...... royal yacht
(people): ...... travel habits
(the Greenes): ...... mountain lodge
(women): ...... travel magazines
(the boss): ...... travel itinerary
(Jack and Jill): ...... adventure
(students): ...... field trip
(children): ...... passports
(politicians): ...... travel expenses

sections.title: I: Possessive determiners and pronouns
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in the box. Use each word only once.

items.sentence: Tell Sofia that it's my booking, not .......
Are you sure this travel card isn't ...... because it looks just like the one I was given at check-in?
Thanks for the advice, but I think I'll plan ...... own route.
I found a guidebook on the hostel floor next to some tourists and asked them if it was .......
Remind your travel companion to bring ...... sunscreen to the beach tomorrow.
I'm not sure, but I think ...... the ferry terminal, isn't it?
Could you ask the other campers not to leave ...... rubbish at my pitch again, please?
What ancient secrets do you think the jungle might be hiding within ...... depths?

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: If a line is correct, click OK. If there is an extra word, click it.

items.sentence: Well, Maria, that's my **own** first day of the expedition out of the way.
I wasn't sure what to expect, but it turned out to be incredible.
Our guide was really knowledgeable, much more so than yours **guide** sounds from your postcard.
No sooner had I arrived at the hostel than **did** he show me to my room!
It was a really wonderful surprise.
Not only did he do that, but he was also kind enough to show me all the best local spots.
The hostel room is really spacious and **its** comfortable.
The other guests all have their own small desks in their rooms, but mine **desk** still has not arrived, so I am sharing with someone else for now.
Little did I **not** realise when I booked this trip that I was going to enjoy it so much.
I have finally found a destination that I am truly happy in, and so will you [have], I'm sure.
